(大山咋神) is a [kami](/wiki/Kami "Kami") highly significant in the [Japanese religion](/wiki/Japanese_religion "Japanese religion") of [Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō](/wiki/Sann%C5%8D_Ichijitsu_Shint%C5%8D "Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō"),[[1]](#cite_note-sugahara-shinkai-菅原信海-the-distinctive-features-of-sanno-ichijitsu-shinto-1996a-1) and worshipped in the [Hiyoshi Taisha](/wiki/Hiyoshi_Taisha "Hiyoshi Taisha") network of shrines[[2]](#cite_note-religions-mdpi-2-2) and the [Matsunoo Taisha](/wiki/Matsunoo_Taisha "Matsunoo Taisha") network.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) They are also known as Sannō Gongen ([山王権現](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%B1%B1%E7%8E%8B%E6%A8%A9%E7%8F%BE "ja:山王権現")) in a Buddhist context.[[5]](#cite_note-the-japan-times-2004a-5) They are the child of [Toshigami](/wiki/Toshigami "Toshigami")[[6]](#cite_note-6) and grandson of [Susanoo](/wiki/Susanoo "Susanoo").[[7]](#cite_note-7) Oyamakui no Kami is considered to be androgynous and possibly hermaphroditic.[[8]](#cite_note-8) They are the kami of mountains and good health.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Shinto-Buddhist deity

| Oyamakui no Kami | |
| --- | --- |
| [Sanno Gongen](#Sanno_Gongen) Hall ([Makiyama Hannyuin Chou Temple](/w/index.php?title=Choufukuji_Temple_(Mimasaka_City)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Choufukuji Temple (Mimasaka City) (page does not exist)") [[ja](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E9%95%B7%E7%A6%8F%E5%AF%BA_(%E7%BE%8E%E4%BD%9C%E5%B8%82) "ja:長福寺 (美作市)")]) | |
| Major cult centre | [Hiyoshi Taisha](/wiki/Hiyoshi_Taisha "Hiyoshi Taisha")  [Matsunoo Taisha](/wiki/Matsunoo_Taisha "Matsunoo Taisha") |
| Genealogy | |
| Parents | * [Toshigami](/wiki/Toshigami "Toshigami") (father) |

**Ōyamakui no Kami** (大山咋神) is a [kami](/wiki/Kami "Kami") highly significant in the [Japanese religion](/wiki/Japanese_religion "Japanese religion") of [Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō](/wiki/Sann%C5%8D_Ichijitsu_Shint%C5%8D "Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō"),[[1]](#cite_note-sugahara-shinkai-菅原信海-the-distinctive-features-of-sanno-ichijitsu-shinto-1996a-1) and worshipped in the [Hiyoshi Taisha](/wiki/Hiyoshi_Taisha "Hiyoshi Taisha") network of shrines[[2]](#cite_note-religions-mdpi-2-2) and the [Matsunoo Taisha](/wiki/Matsunoo_Taisha "Matsunoo Taisha") network.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) They are also known as Sannō Gongen ([山王権現](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%B1%B1%E7%8E%8B%E6%A8%A9%E7%8F%BE "ja:山王権現")) in a Buddhist context.[[5]](#cite_note-the-japan-times-2004a-5) They are the child of [Toshigami](/wiki/Toshigami "Toshigami")[[6]](#cite_note-6) and grandson of [Susanoo](/wiki/Susanoo "Susanoo").[[7]](#cite_note-7) Oyamakui no Kami is considered to be androgynous and possibly hermaphroditic.[[8]](#cite_note-8) They are the kami of mountains and good health.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Due to their prominence at [Hiyoshi Taisha](/wiki/Hiyoshi_Taisha "Hiyoshi Taisha"), the [Chinjusha](/wiki/Chinjusha "Chinjusha") of [Enryaku-ji](/wiki/Enryaku-ji "Enryaku-ji"),[[10]](#cite_note-10) the head temple of [Tendai](/wiki/Tendai "Tendai")[[11]](#cite_note-11) and the shrine itself leading [Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō](/wiki/Sann%C5%8D_Ichijitsu_Shint%C5%8D "Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō"), they have a very prominent role in the sect, often in the consolidated divinity of Sannō Gongen.[[1]](#cite_note-sugahara-shinkai-菅原信海-the-distinctive-features-of-sanno-ichijitsu-shinto-1996a-1)

> Sannō Gongen is the archetypal deity of heaven, earth, and human beings, the spirit dwelling in the green of the willow, the red of the blossom. This is a truth transmitted from mind to mind

— Kenmitsu naishogi[[1]](#cite_note-sugahara-shinkai-菅原信海-the-distinctive-features-of-sanno-ichijitsu-shinto-1996a-1): 67

The [Kojiki](/wiki/Kojiki "Kojiki") says they live on [Mount Hiei](/wiki/Mount_Hiei "Mount Hiei").[[12]](#cite_note-12) He is also worshipped at [Hie Shrine](/wiki/Hie_Shrine "Hie Shrine").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oyamakui_no_Kami&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

There is debate about the origin of the kami with some saying they originated in [Matsunoo Taisha](/wiki/Matsunoo_Taisha "Matsunoo Taisha") and others saying they originated in [Hiyoshi Taisha](/wiki/Hiyoshi_Taisha "Hiyoshi Taisha").[[2]](#cite_note-religions-mdpi-2-2)

The first mention of the *[kami](/wiki/Kami "Kami")* Oyamakui is recorded in [Kojiki](/wiki/Kojiki "Kojiki"), written in the 8th century AD, which states that this god resides at [Mount Hiei](/wiki/Mount_Hiei "Mount Hiei"), which is located immediately to the west of Hiyoshi Taisha. This *kami* was relocated from the summit of the mountain to their present location in the seventh year of the reign of the semi-legendary [Emperor Sujin](/wiki/Emperor_Sujin "Emperor Sujin"), or 90 BC per the traditional calendar. In 668 AD, [Emperor Tenji](/wiki/Emperor_Tenji "Emperor Tenji") decided to relocate the capital to [Ōmi Province](/wiki/%C5%8Cmi_Province "Ōmi Province") and built the [Ōtsu Palace](/wiki/%C5%8Ctsu_Palace "Ōtsu Palace"). At this time, the *kami* of [Ōmiwa Shrine](/wiki/%C5%8Cmiwa_Shrine "Ōmiwa Shrine") in [Yamato Province](/wiki/Yamato_Province "Yamato Province") (who served as protector of the imperial dynasty) was relocated as well, and was installed in the Nishi Hongū, whereas the original sanctuary came to be called the Higashi Hongū. In 788 AD, [Saichō](/wiki/Saich%C5%8D "Saichō") erected the [Tendai](/wiki/Tendai "Tendai") [Buddhist temple](/wiki/Japanese_Buddhism "Japanese Buddhism") complex of [Enryaku-ji](/wiki/Enryaku-ji "Enryaku-ji") on Mount Hiei. After the transfer of the capital to [Heian-kyō](/wiki/Heian-ky%C5%8D "Heian-kyō"), Enryaku-ji and by extension, Hiyoshi Taisha came to be guardians of the spiritually vulnerable northeast quadrant from the capital. As Enryaku-ji became ever more powerful, and the Buddhist faith gradually amalgamated with Shinto under the *[Shinbutsu-shūgō](/wiki/Shinbutsu-sh%C5%ABg%C5%8D "Shinbutsu-shūgō")* policy, Hiyoshi Taisha was subsumed into Enryaku-ji. As missionaries from Enryaku-ji built Buddhist temples all across Japan, they also spread the faith in the "[Sanno Gongen](#Sanno_Gongen)" and the Hie *kami*.

The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early [Heian period](/wiki/Heian_period "Heian period").[[14]](#cite_note-14) In 965, [Emperor Murakami](/wiki/Emperor_Murakami "Emperor Murakami") ordered that Imperial messengers were sent to report important events to the guardian *kami* of Japan,[[15]](#cite_note-pf1182-15) and Hie Taisha was added to this listing by [Emperor Go-Suzaku](/wiki/Emperor_Go-Suzaku "Emperor Go-Suzaku") in 1039. This unique number of Imperial-designated shrines has not been altered since that time.[[15]](#cite_note-pf1182-15)

[Tenkai](/wiki/Tenkai "Tenkai") wrote extensively about Sanno Gongen. He says Sanno Gongen is very important. According to Tenkai, all kami come from Sanno Gongen. Kami are like parts of Sanno Gongen. Tenkai uses many quotes to explain this. One quote is from the Kenmitsu naishdgi. It says Sanno Gongen is a key deity. Another quote is from the Nimon sosokushu. It says Sanno Gongen represents all dharmas. A different source mentions [Jūzenji](/wiki/J%C5%ABzenji "Jūzenji"). It says [Jūzenji](/wiki/J%C5%ABzenji "Jūzenji") is part of heaven and earth. [Jūzenji](/wiki/J%C5%ABzenji "Jūzenji") exists with all beings. The Masafusa-ki is another text. Oe Masafusa wrote it. He lived from 1041 to 1111. This text also talks about Sanno Gongen. It says Sanno Gongen is the source of all Japanese kami.[[1]](#cite_note-sugahara-shinkai-菅原信海-the-distinctive-features-of-sanno-ichijitsu-shinto-1996a-1): 68–69

[Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi") had a deep faith in the [Sanno Gongen](#Sanno_Gongen), as his childhood name was "Hiyoshi Maru" and his nickname was "monkey", an animal which was considered to be the [spiritual messenger](/wiki/Shinshi "Shinshi") of the Hie *kami*. The Nishi Hongū was reconstructed in 1586 and the Higashi Hongū in 1595. [Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu "Tokugawa Ieyasu") also had faith in the [Sanno Gongen](#Sanno_Gongen) and the shrine was supported by the [Tokugawa shogunate](/wiki/Tokugawa_shogunate "Tokugawa shogunate").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

## Sannō Gongen

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Oyamakui_no_Kami&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Sannō Gongen")]

See also: [Gongen](/wiki/Gongen "Gongen")

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Translation_to_english_arrow.svg) | You can help **expand this section with text translated from [the corresponding article](https://ja.wikipedia.org/wiki/%E5%B1%B1%E7%8E%8B%E6%A8%A9%E7%8F%BE "ja:山王権現") in Japanese**. *(November 2023)* Click [show] for important translation instructions.  * [View](https://translate.google.com/translate?&u=https%3A%2F%2Fja.wikipedia.org%2Fwiki%2F%E5%B1%B1%E7%8E%8B%E6%A8%A9%E7%8F%BE&sl=ja&tl=en&prev=_t&hl=en) a machine-translated version of the Japanese article. * Machine translation, like [DeepL](https://deepl.com) or [Google Translate](https://translate.google.com/), is a useful starting point for translations, but translators must revise errors as necessary and confirm that the translation is accurate, rather than simply copy-pasting machine-translated text into the English Wikipedia. * Do not translate text that appears unreliable or low-quality. If possible, verify the text with references provided in the foreign-language article. * You **must** provide [copyright attribution](/wiki/Wikipedia:Copying_within_Wikipedia "Wikipedia:Copying within Wikipedia") in the [edit summary](/wiki/Help:Edit_summary "Help:Edit summary") accompanying your translation by providing an [interlanguage link](/wiki/Help:Interlanguage_links "Help:Interlanguage links") to the source of your translation. A model attribution edit summary is `Content in this edit is translated from the existing Japanese Wikipedia article at [[:ja:山王権現]]; see its history for attribution.` * You may also add the template `{{Translated|ja|山王権現}}` to the [talk page](/wiki/Talk:Oyamakui_no_Kami "Talk:Oyamakui no Kami"). * For more guidance, see [Wikipedia:Translation](/wiki/Wikipedia:Translation "Wikipedia:Translation"). |

*Sannō Gongen* (山王権現) is the Buddhist name of this deity.[[5]](#cite_note-the-japan-times-2004a-5)

[Jūzenji](/wiki/J%C5%ABzenji "Jūzenji") was worshipped as one of the seven key emanations of Sannō Gongen.[[17]](#cite_note-porath-2022a-17) He was at times seen as being the core god which all things emanated from.[[17]](#cite_note-porath-2022a-17)
